solution: For the repeated linear factor, \( \int \frac{[[c]]}{(x+[[a]])^{2}}\,dx = -\frac{[[c]]}{x+[[a]]} \). Evaluating from \( 0 \) to \( [[b]] \) gives \( \frac{[[c]]}{[[a]]} - \frac{[[c]]}{[[a]]+[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of \( [[b]] \) in the numerator of the combined fraction.
This treats the antiderivative as \( \tfrac{[[c]]\cdot[[b]]}{[[a]]^{2}} \), a plug-in error.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign, as if the antiderivative were \( +\tfrac{[[c]]}{x+[[a]]} \).

stem: Here the repeated linear factor has a LINEAR numerator, so the decomposition needs two distinct terms \( \dfrac{A}{x+[[a]]} + \dfrac{B}{(x+[[a]])^{2}} \). Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[b]]} \frac{[[d]]x + [[e]]}{\left(x + [[a]]\right)^{2}}\,dx \).
solution: Write \( [[d]]x+[[e]] = [[d]](x+[[a]]) + ([[e]]-[[d]]\cdot[[a]]) \), so the integrand splits as \( \dfrac{[[d]]}{x+[[a]]} + \dfrac{[[e]]-[[d]]\cdot[[a]]}{(x+[[a]])^{2}} \). Integrating gives \( [[d]]\ln(x+[[a]]) - \dfrac{[[e]]-[[d]]\cdot[[a]]}{x+[[a]]} \), evaluated from \( 0 \) to \( [[b]] \), equal to \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the logarithmic term from the \( A/(x+a) \) piece and drops the algebraic \( B/(x+a)^2 \) contribution entirely.
This keeps only the algebraic \( B/(x+a)^2 \) piece (using the wrong constant \( e \) instead of \( e-da \)) and drops the logarithm entirely.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: The denominator factors as a distinct linear piece times an irreducible quadratic (it has no real roots since \( q > 0 \)), calling for the decomposition \( \dfrac{A}{x+p} + \dfrac{Bx+C}{x^{2}+q} \). Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[hi]]} \frac{[[c]]}{\left(x + [[p]]\right)\left(x^{2} + [[q]]\right)}\,dx \).
solution: Decomposing \( \dfrac{1}{(x+[[p]])(x^{2}+[[q]])} = \dfrac{A}{x+[[p]]} + \dfrac{Bx+C}{x^{2}+[[q]]} \) and integrating (a logarithm from the linear factor, a log-plus-arctangent pair from the quadratic factor) gives, after scaling by \( [[c]] \), \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This plugs \( x=0 \) into the factored denominator instead of actually integrating, a common shortcut error.
This multiplies that same plug-in error by the upper limit, another shortcut error.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: The irreducible quadratic \( x^{2} + [[asq]] \) has no real roots, so \( \dfrac{[[c]]}{x^{2}+[[asq]]} \) integrates directly to an arctangent (no partial-fraction split is needed). Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[a]]} \frac{[[c]]}{x^{2} + [[asq]]}\,dx \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \pi/2 \) (as if \( \arctan(\infty) \)) instead of \( \arctan(1)=\pi/4 \).
This drops the leading \( 1/a \) coefficient's effect and uses \( \pi \) directly instead of \( \pi/4 \).
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: Split \( \dfrac{[[d]]x + [[e]]}{x^{2}+[[asq]]} \) into a piece that integrates to a logarithm (from the \( x \)-term) and a piece that integrates to an arctangent (from the constant term). Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[a]]} \frac{[[d]]x + [[e]]}{x^{2} + [[asq]]}\,dx \).
solution: Split the numerator: \( \dfrac{[[d]]x}{x^{2}+[[asq]]} \) integrates (via \( u=x^2+[[asq]] \)) to \( \tfrac{[[d]]}{2}\ln(x^{2}+[[asq]]) \), and \( \dfrac{[[e]]}{x^{2}+[[asq]]} \) integrates to \( \tfrac{[[e]]}{[[a]]}\arctan\tfrac{x}{[[a]]} \). Evaluating both from \( 0 \) to \( [[a]] \) and adding gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the arctangent piece from the constant-numerator term and drops the logarithmic piece entirely.
This keeps only the logarithmic piece from the \( x \)-numerator term and drops the arctangent piece entirely.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: The irreducible quadratic factor \( x^{2}+[[asq]] \) is now SQUARED in the denominator, so a trigonometric substitution \( x = [[a]]\tan\theta \) is needed rather than a direct arctangent formula. Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[a]]} \frac{[[c]]}{\left(x^{2} + [[asq]]\right)^{2}}\,dx \).
solution: With \( x=[[a]]\tan\theta \), \( x^{2}+[[asq]] = [[asq]]\sec^{2}\theta \) and \( dx=[[a]]\sec^{2}\theta\,d\theta \), so the integral becomes \( \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[a]]^{3}}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\pi/4}\cos^{2}\theta\,d\theta = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[a]]^{3}}\left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}+\dfrac{1}{4}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses only the plain arctangent formula for a single (unsquared) irreducible quadratic factor.
This applies the reduction formula but drops the algebraic \( 1/4 \) piece, keeping only the \( \pi/8 \) sector term.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
